---
title: "Assignment 1"
---

My first assignment has two parts.

## (a)

**Summary of the Conversation with Cem Vardar**

In this video, I watched a very insightful interview with Cem Vardar about how our department, Industrial Engineering (IE), fits into the world of Data Science. As an IE student, it was encouraging to hear that we actually have a huge head start. Cem Vardar explains that while anyone can learn to code, our "IE mindset" is what really matters. He says that we are trained to look at the whole system and focus on optimization and decision-making.

The main takeaway for me was that Data Science isn't just about playing with data; it's about solving real business problems and turning raw information into actionable insights. Cem Vardar also talks about his own career, showing how the math and statistics we learn in class are the building blocks for high-level technologies like Machine Learning. It made me realize that being an engineer who understands the "why" behind the data is just as important as being a coder who understands the "how."

**Open-Ended Question**

-   **Question:** What is the most important skill an Industrial Engineer brings to a Data Science team according to Cem Vardar?

    -   **Answer:** It is their ability to apply "systems thinking" and optimization techniques to ensure that the data analysis actually leads to better business decisions.

**Multiple-Choice Question**

-   **Question:** According to the talk, why is Industrial Engineering a good fit for Data Science?
    a)  Because IEs only care about manual calculations.
    b)  Because IEs focus on transforming data into insights for better decision-making.
    c)  Because IEs don't need to use computers at all.
    d)  Because Data Science is only about memorizing R code.
-   **Answer:** **b) Because IEs focus on transforming data into insights for better decision-making.**

## (b)

```{r}
my_first <- "Mevlut"
my_birth_year <- 2003

k <- (nchar(my_first) + my_birth_year) %% 15 + 8
print(k)
  library(dslabs)
data("polls_us_election_2016")
if (k %% 2 == 0) {
  head(polls_us_election_2016, k)
} else {
  tail(polls_us_election_2016, k)
}
sum(is.na(polls_us_election_2016))
na_per_column <- sort(colSums(is.na(polls_us_election_2016)), decreasing = TRUE)
head(na_per_column, 8)
polls_new <- polls_us_election_2016
for(i in 1:ncol(polls_new)) { #this part of the code was created by the help of ai.
  if(is.numeric(polls_new[[i]])) {
    polls_new[[i]][is.na(polls_new[[i]])] <- my_birth_year + k
  } else {
    polls_new[[i]] <- as.character(polls_new[[i]])
    polls_new[[i]][is.na(polls_new[[i]])] <- paste0(my_first,'_' ,k)
  }
}
if (k %% 2 == 0) {
  head(polls_new, k)
} else {
  tail(polls_new, k)
}

print(sum(is.na(polls_new)))
print(anyNA(polls_new))
#**AI Prompt for NA Replacement:** "How can I write an R loop to iterate through all columns of a data frame and replace NA values with a specific number for numeric columns, and a specific string for character/factor columns without overwriting the original data set?"
```
